1:
The chapter opens on Absalom's war-council, and the witnesses read Ahithophel's opening counsel as a masterstroke of ruthless timing. The grammatical-historical commentators fix on the word this night. Ellicott judges that Ahithophel saw clearly how everything turned on striking an immediate blow: David, in his distress and weariness, was in no condition to resist a sudden onset, and the proof that the counsel was shrewd is the very anxiety of Hushai to overturn it and to send warning to the king with all speed. He records the one difficulty of chronology — this night is usually taken of the night David left Jerusalem, yet from the later notice (17:16) and the earlier word about the fords (15:28) it appears David was already encamped by the Jordan, a greater distance than one day's march would cover.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke measures the danger with a soldier's eye: had this counsel been followed, David and his little troop would soon have been destroyed, and nothing but the miraculous interposition of God could have saved them — twelve thousand chosen men, falling on him in his wholly unprepared state, would have settled the business of the kingdom at a stroke. Ahithophel, he adds, well saw that with this advice neglected all was lost.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the scene into the realm of providence and type. He marvels that the body of Israel, to whom David had been so great a blessing, should join a profligate son thirsting after his own father's blood, and notes that the resolve to cut David off passed, for aught that appears, unanimously, none daring to ask what evil he had done. Ahithophel's plan is to 'smite the shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered' — to include the whole war in a little compass by falling on the king only. Henry sets beside it the plot of Caiaphas, 'that second Ahithophel,' who reasoned that one man should die for the people (John 11:50), and the murderers who said, 'Kill the heir, and the inheritance shall be ours' (Matt. 21:38); yet the counsel of them both was turned into foolishness. The reformed John Gill simply anchors the timing, noting that Ahithophel spoke thus either at the same council or quickly after his former advice. The witnesses agree: the advice was deadly precisely because it was sound, and only God's overruling would blunt it.
2:
Ahithophel unfolds his method, and the witnesses dwell on its cold efficiency and on the hidden hand behind it. The grammatical-historical commentators sharpen the picture of the ambush. Ellicott protests that the English 'and will make him afraid' is hardly strong enough; the thought is that Ahithophel would throw David's band into sheer panic by a sudden night attack, and in the confusion easily secure the person of the king. Keil and Delitzsch fill in the plan verse by verse — to come upon him 'weary and weak-handed,' to scatter all the people, and to smite the king only, so that the man Absalom seeks would be as if all had returned and all the people be at peace — and they observe that this very saying pleased Absalom and all the elders of Israel, yet Absalom still sent for Hushai to hear his opinion.

The reformed Gill reads the tactician's intent: Ahithophel would come upon David while he was weary with travelling and with grief, when he was seeking a little rest by sleep, and so surprise him unawares, in no posture of defence. The horror of the design is that it is entirely plausible; the plan needs only speed to succeed.

It is the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley who lifts the eye from the tactics to the theology. That such a wretch as Absalom should aim at his father's throat is not strange, he grants; but that the body of the people, to whom David had been so great a blessing, should join with him in it is amazing — and here Wesley finds the decisive clue: 'the finger of God was in it.' David had provoked God to turn against him, and this fair and murderous counsel is the rod. Wesley draws the pastoral warning that the best of parents and the best of princes must not think it strange if they are injured by those who should be their support and joy, when, like David, they have first grieved their God. So the voices do not divide: the plan is militarily sound, spiritually it is a chastisement, and its aim — to kill 'the king only' and call the resulting ruin a general peace — is the usurper's counterfeit of reconciliation, which the next verse makes plainer still.
3:
The witnesses read the close of Ahithophel's speech as the mask of a usurper — a promise of peace that is really a promise of murder. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke states the logic bare: 'Only secure David, and all Israel will be on thy side. He is the soul of the whole; destroy him, and all the rest will submit.' The single life of the king is the hinge on which the entire kingdom turns, so the whole rebellion may be won or lost in one night's work. The very brevity of the calculation is its horror — a whole nation's allegiance reduced to the killing of a single man, and that man the counsellor's own former king, whom he had served at the council-board for years.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott exposes the artful flattery folded into the words. In saying he will 'bring back all the people' to Absalom, this evil counsellor quietly assumes that Absalom is already the rightful king and that the people who followed David have merely strayed from their true allegiance and need only be recalled to it. The rhetoric grants Absalom his stolen throne before a blow is struck. Keil and Delitzsch trace the same movement in the Hebrew — the man whom Absalom seeks being struck down, all the people would be brought back and 'all the people shall be in peace' — and note again that the saying pleased Absalom and all the elders of Israel.

The reformed Gill clarifies what Ahithophel does and does not intend. He is not proposing to take David's followers prisoner and drag them back, for he had just offered to let them scatter and escape; rather he means to reduce all Israel to Absalom's obedience at once by this single decisive stroke. The chilling word in the mouth of the counsellor is peace: as Matthew Henry observes elsewhere in the chapter, a general ruin is here christened a general peace, for so the usurper — like the strong man armed who keeps his palace — calls the quiet of a conquered land. The witnesses are one: the counsel is seductive because it wraps a father's blood in the language of national repose, and it is exactly this plausibility that God must overthrow if David is to be spared.
4:
The verse records only that 'the saying pleased Absalom well, and all the elders of Israel,' but the reformed Gill finds in that brief notice two distinct wonders, and the witnesses let him draw them out. The first is that the plan itself was, on its own terms, well contrived — a scheme to surprise David and his men by night in the exhausted condition they were in; and, as Gill remarks, there is nothing like seizing an opportunity and making quick dispatch in executing a design of this kind. On the level of tactics the council had every reason to be pleased.

The second and greater wonder lies in who is pleased. Gill confesses it is strange that Absalom could so easily fall in with a plan to take away the life of so indulgent a father, a thing to be accounted for only by the blindness of his ambition. But it is stranger still, he presses, that the elders of Israel should be pleased with it and should agree to and determine upon the death of a prince who had for so many years ruled them with justice and equity, clemency and mercy, who had fought their battles for them and raised their nation to a pitch of glory it had never known before. That such men should sit as a privy council and resolve on regicide against their greatest benefactor is a measure of how far a nation may be carried once it has cast off its true king.

The immediate context deepens the point. Ahithophel had asked only to 'smite the king only,' and the gladness of Absalom at that very phrase (as the wider narrative of vv.1–4 shows) betrays a heart from which natural affection has been wholly emptied. Yet the same verse holds the seed of the rebellion's undoing, for it adds that Absalom nonetheless sent for Hushai; the unanimous applause of the elders did not still the restlessness that God had planted in the usurper's mind. The one witness here teaches what the chapter will confirm: sin can win the approval of a whole council and still be marching, all the while, toward the ruin that a righteous providence has appointed for it.
5:
Absalom's call for Hushai looks like a small procedural courtesy, but the witnesses read it as the visible turning-point of the whole rebellion, and they trace it to the hand of God. The reformed Gill marks it as 'something very remarkable in the providence of God': that Absalom should wish to hear Hushai at all — when Ahithophel's counsel had been universally approved, and when Hushai was well known to have been David's intimate friend and confidant, neither so settled nor so sworn in Absalom's interest as Ahithophel — can be ascribed only to the will of God, working to defeat Ahithophel's counsel by blinding the mind of Absalom and inclining it to prefer a lesser voice.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley says the same with even greater force. It is 'a wonderful effect of Divine Providence, influencing his heart, that he could not rest in Ahithophel's counsel,' though it was so evidently wise and approved by the general consent of his whole party, and that he should crave the advice of Hushai, whose reputation for wisdom was not equal to Ahithophel's and who had as yet given no proof of fidelity to the new regime. Wesley lays down the settled principle: 'there is no contending with that God who can arm a man against himself, and destroy him by his own mistakes and passions.'

The grammatical-historical commentators read the moment more soberly at the surface while leaving room for the deeper cause. Ellicott takes it as the good sense of Absalom and all the people, who at first at once approved Ahithophel's counsel, yet at so important a crisis wished to consult the other famous counsellor of David's court as well. Keil and Delitzsch record the exchange plainly: Absalom asks Hushai whether they should follow Ahithophel's advice or not, and Hushai answers, 'The advice is not good that Ahithophel hath given this time.' Beneath the courtly deliberation the schools agree on the real engine of events: the same providence that had turned Ahithophel's earlier counsel to David's chastening now moved to turn this counsel aside, so that the fugitive king might be spared.
6:
The narrative gives only a hinge — 'and when Hushai was come to Absalom' — and the reformed Gill supplies the single, quiet observation that Hushai came in, very probably, into the council chamber itself. Small as the notice is, it marks a decisive change of place and of players: the friend of David is now standing inside the very cabinet of the rebellion, admitted to the secret deliberations where his master's life is being weighed. Hushai had been sent back into the city by David himself for exactly this service, charged to defeat Ahithophel's counsel from within (15:34), and this quiet sentence records him taking up his post.

The verse's own words press the drama further, for Absalom does not merely ask Hushai to speak; he lays Ahithophel's plan before him and demands a verdict — 'Ahithophel hath spoken after this manner: shall we do after his saying? if not, speak thou.' The usurper thus invites the one man in the room most bound to David to sit as arbiter between two courses, one of which would end David's life that night. Everything now hangs on whether Hushai can, without betraying himself, make the ruinous counsel appear the worse. The reader who recalls David's prayer that the Lord would 'turn the counsel of Ahithophel into foolishness' (15:31) sees the answer being set in motion in this bare procedural sentence.

Gill's brevity is itself instructive. Where the source material is thin, the witness does not speculate or embellish; he simply situates the scene and leaves the weight to fall on what follows. The placing of Hushai in the council is the providential opportunity for which David's friend had returned to Jerusalem at the king's own charge, sent back precisely that he might 'defeat the counsel of Ahithophel' from within. The single reformed voice therefore reads this verse as the moment the trap is set: not by force, but by a counsellor's word spoken at the right instant in the right room, God is about to overthrow the wisest head in Israel. What the verse states, the chapter will vindicate — that the safety of the Lord's anointed could turn on so slight a thing as an invitation to speak.
7:
Hushai opens his counter-speech, and the witnesses admire the craft with which he must overturn a revered adviser without seeming to. The grammatical-historical Ellicott catches the cunning of a single phrase. Hushai does not say Ahithophel's counsel is bad; he says the counsel is 'not good at this time' — and Ellicott would even transpose the words to bring out the sense, 'This time the counsel of Ahithophel is not good.' The implication is a stroke of policy: by conceding that Ahithophel's previous advice had been wise, Hushai assumes an air of candour and impartial judgement, so that his dissent sounds like the reluctant honesty of a man who usually agrees.

Keil and Delitzsch lay out the substance of the argument Hushai is beginning to build. He appeals to what Absalom himself must know of his father and his father's men — that they are heroes, and of a ferocious temper, 'like a bear in the field robbed of her young' — and that David is a seasoned man of war who will not lodge for the night among the common people, so that the hope of catching him asleep and unguarded is a delusion. Every clause is chosen to make Ahithophel's swift night-raid look reckless rather than decisive.

The reformed Gill fills in the same opening move from David's side of the ledger. Having been given leave to speak his mind freely, Hushai answers Ahithophel's picture of a weary, weak-handed king with the opposite portrait: David and his men are not the easily-frightened stragglers Ahithophel had imagined, but men of courage and valour who will not be scattered by a single sudden blow. Gill marks that Hushai spoke only when given leave, and with all the deference due to Ahithophel's settled reputation, so that his dissent should carry the more weight with the council. The witnesses do not divide; together they show a loyal counsellor turning the council's own knowledge of David's prowess into an argument for delay — the delay that, unknown to Absalom, is precisely what David needs. What looks like sober military caution is in truth the opening of the snare, and the reader is meant to feel how thin the thread was on which the anointed king's deliverance hung.
8:
Hushai presses his portrait of David and his men, and the witnesses gather around his central image — the robbed bear. The reformed Gill states the plain rhetorical purpose: 'thou knowest thy father and his men, that they be mighty men' — men of courage and valour, not the weak-handed and faint-hearted band, easily intimidated, that Ahithophel had made them out to be. The whole weight of the speech is to reverse Ahithophel's estimate, to make formidable what he had made contemptible.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lingers on the figure 'as a bear robbed of her whelps,' observing that all wild beasts are furious when robbed of their young, and that there are remarkable instances of the maternal ferocity of the bear in just such straits — he even reserves a long account of one for the end of the chapter. The comparison is exact: David's veterans, stung by the treason done to their king, would fight not merely with courage but with the blind, unstoppable rage of a creature bereaved. Keil and Delitzsch add the further clause that Hushai builds on it — that David, 'a man of war,' will not pass the night with the people, so that no sleeping camp lies open to a surprise, and the raider would find not a huddle of the weary but a lion in ambush.

Underneath the vivid picture the grammatical-historical reading keeps the psychological point in view: Hushai is not simply describing David's men, he is playing on the fear such a description breeds in untried troops. A bear robbed of her young is a proverb in Scripture itself for a blind and dangerous fury (compare Prov. 17:12), which lends Hushai's chosen figure its unsettling force. All that he says of their bravery and hardened experience is, as the commentators grant, entirely true; the deceit lies only in the use he makes of it — not to warn Absalom off a real danger, but to buy David time. The witnesses stand together: the robbed bear is the emblem of a loyalty turned fierce, and Hushai wields a truth about David's men as the first lever by which he will pry the council away from the swift stroke that would have destroyed them.
9:
Hushai turns from the men to their captain's cunning, and the witnesses follow him into the picture of David the old fugitive who knows every hiding-place in the land. The reformed Gill draws on David's history: he was long accustomed to such hidden places and well acquainted with them from the days when he fled from Saul, so that Absalom's host would never take him as easily as Ahithophel supposed. Worse, Gill notes, the danger runs both ways — David lying concealed with his men in ambush might at any moment burst from his lurking-place and fall upon some of Absalom's troops unawares.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley expands the same scenario into its psychological chain. David, having so often been driven to such shifts in Saul's time, will lie hidden in some pit unknown to the pursuers, and with his chosen men will wait in ambush until a fit moment, then suddenly come forth and surprise Absalom's soldiers when they least expect it — and probably rout them at the first shock. And here Wesley catches Hushai's cleverest turn: a good success at the very outset, even against a small party, would 'mightily animate David's men to proceed vigorously' and would as surely 'intimidate Absalom's army,' so that the first small defeat would be both the presage and the occasion of a total one.

The grammatical-historical reading confirms that this is the argument's hidden hinge. Hushai has quietly shifted the question from David's weakness to the morale of Absalom's raw levies: it is not that David cannot be beaten, but that a single early reverse, magnified by rumour, would melt an untested army. Gill adds that David, driven to such shifts in the days of Saul, knew the caves and pits of the wilderness of Judah as few men did, so the picture carried a weight of remembered fact that made it the harder for the council to dismiss. The witnesses do not contend here; together they show how skilfully the counsellor works on fear, turning David's very flight and long experience of exile into arguments for caution. The plausible picture of the hidden king in the pit is one more thread in the net Hushai is drawing over the council, and the reader sees that what will save David is not his sword but the timely word of a friend.
10:
Hushai completes the fearful picture with the melting of the bravest heart, and the reformed Gill unfolds the single verse at length. The sense, he explains, is that whoever should hear of Absalom's forces — or even a part of them — being routed would be intimidated, however courageous he might be, however lion-like his temper; and Gill presses the point that even Ahithophel himself, for all his boasted resolve, would find his heart fail and melt like water should he meet such a rebuff at the very outset. The argument is the crown of Hushai's speech: not merely will David be hard to catch, but the report of one reverse will dissolve the courage of the whole rebel army.

Gill dwells on the emblem of the lion, and the witnesses let him. The lion is renowned as much for courage as for strength; it has not only a fierce countenance — whence Scripture can speak of lion-like men in their faces — but a stout heart, so that some have thought the Hebrew name for the beast, labi, akin to the word for heart, leb, and the poets gave their heroes 'the heart of a lion.' To say that such a heart 'shall utterly melt' is to promise the collapse of the very bravest, and so to make Ahithophel's confident stroke look like the prelude to a general panic.

Gill even balances the picture with a curiosity of natural history: some lions of Africa, he reports, are by nature so timid that they flee at the cry of children, so that the men of one region made a proverb of calling blustering cowards 'the lions of Agla.' The touch is not idle; it quietly underscores Hushai's whole strategy, which is to persuade the council that a reputation for ferocity can crumble in an instant once the tide turns. The single reformed witness therefore reads the verse as the argument's finishing blow: Hushai grants the council all its lions, and then tells them that lions melt. Beneath the rhetoric lies the truth the chapter is pressing — that courage and counsel alike are in the hand of the God who was even now appointing the good advice of Ahithophel to be defeated.
11:
Having dismantled Ahithophel's plan, Hushai lays down his own, and the witnesses expose it as a masterpiece of flattery dressed as prudence. The grammatical-historical Ellicott frames the difficulty of Hushai's task — he had not only to make the worse appear the better reason, but to do it in the teeth of a counsellor famous for wisdom and devoted to Absalom, while his own fidelity had just been called in question. He accomplishes it, Ellicott shows, by magnifying every hazard of Ahithophel's swift raid and proposing instead a plan attended with no risk — on the supposition, which he assumes with delicate flattery as already true, that the whole mass of Israel is and will remain on Absalom's side. Keil and Delitzsch give the terms: let all Israel be gathered from Dan to Beersheba, numerous as the sand by the sea, and let Absalom go into the war in his own person.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads the double motive with precision. Hushai's pretence was that they might make sure, though slow, work; his real design was to gain David time to increase his army and better provide for battle, and to let the present heat of the people cool so that they might bethink themselves of their duty and return to their allegiance. And the counsel that Absalom lead the host himself is pure bait: his presence, Wesley notes, would put life and courage into the soldiers, and 'the glory of the victory will be wholly thine' — the very glory Ahithophel had sought to claim by offering to go without him.

The reformed Gill simply opens the door to the advice with Hushai's own 'Therefore I counsel,' and the witnesses close ranks behind Ellicott's verdict: the plan is worthless as strategy and perfect as manipulation. By feeding Absalom's vanity — the vast muster he may review, the personal command he may enjoy — Hushai makes delay itself desirable to the very man it will destroy. It is a study in how a proud heart is betrayed by being applauded, and how, under the appearance of the safest course, the counsellor secures for David the one thing he needs, which is time.
12:
Hushai promises an overwhelming and inescapable assault, and the witnesses fasten on his chosen image, the falling dew. The reformed Gill states the practical claim first: a host so vast, spread abroad over the country, could not fail to find David out in whatever lurking-place he hid, whereas a mere twelve thousand might well miss him — so the argument answers Ahithophel's earlier boast with its own promise of certainty.

Keil and Delitzsch work at the Hebrew of the figure: 'we let ourselves down upon him, as the dew falls upon the earth,' so that of David and all the men with him 'there shall not be left so much as one.' The wesleyan-arminian Wesley draws out why the comparison is so apt: David and his men would be overwhelmed as the dew covers the ground — that is, 'plenteously, suddenly, irresistibly, and on all sides; for so the dew falls.' The picture is deliberately total; nothing escapes a dew that settles everywhere at once, and the promise is of an annihilation as silent and as universal as the morning moisture.

The grammatical-historical reading notes the calculated contrast Hushai is drawing between the two plans. Ahithophel had offered speed and a single target — the king only; Hushai offers mass and comprehensiveness — every man cut off, none left. It is worth observing, as the commentators do, that this promise to destroy 'all the men that are with him' may have pleased Absalom even more than Ahithophel's narrower aim, for it gratified his hatred of David's friends as well as his ambition. So the witnesses agree that Hushai has matched Ahithophel point for point: where the wise counsellor promised a quick and certain stroke, the loyal one promises a slow and (he says) even more certain flood. The commentators note that this pledge to leave 'not so much as one' answered exactly to Ahithophel's own boast of a swift and total stroke, so that Absalom heard from Hushai the very certainty of success he had heard before, only clothed now in the grander image of an irresistible multitude. Yet the loyal counsellor knows all the while that the muster he describes will never fall like dew at all, because the delay it requires is exactly what will save the king it pretends to doom.
13:
Hushai crowns his counsel with a piece of studied hyperbole — if David flee into a city, all Israel will bring ropes and drag the city itself into the river — and the witnesses debate chiefly how literally the ropes are meant. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take it as frank exaggeration: Hushai speaks in hyperboles of the irresistible power the whole nation would put forth once summoned, dragging the walls down to the brook 'until there be not one small stone found there,' precisely in order to make his plan the more plausible. Ellicott agrees that this is hyperbole to display the overwhelming might of a united Israel, and that it was 'pleasing to the vanity and dazzling to the imagination of Absalom.'

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads it in the same key, as a hyperbolical expression exactly suited to the vain-glorious temper of this insolent young man — implying either that they would do so if no other means served, or that they would be numerous enough to do it should there be need. The image is calculated less to inform strategy than to intoxicate a proud imagination with the sense of limitless force at its command.

Adam Clarke, however, is not content to let the ropes stand as mere rhetoric and opens the one real question of the verse. The Hebrew word chabalim, he notes — the root of our word 'cable' — means ropes, yet it is unlikely any city could actually be pulled down with ropes; and so the Chaldee paraphrase, which he reckons the best judge, renders the word by mashreyan, 'towers,' giving an easier sense. Thus a small textual crux opens beneath the flourish. The reformed Gill keeps the plainer track, simply supposing the case of David sheltering in a strong, fortified place. The witnesses do not finally divide on the point that matters: whether the word be 'ropes' or 'towers,' the clause is Hushai's final dazzling stroke, and its true function is not military but psychological — to sweep the council off its feet, and with it to sweep away the deadly counsel of Ahithophel.
14:
The verse is the theological hinge of the whole chapter, and the witnesses read it with one voice: Absalom and all the men of Israel judged Hushai's counsel better than Ahithophel's, 'for the LORD had appointed to defeat the good counsel of Ahithophel, to the intent that the LORD might bring evil upon Absalom.' The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch locate precisely where the deception lay. All that Hushai had said of David's bravery and his men's heroism was well founded; the fatal falsehood was the assumption that all Israel, from Dan to Beersheba, would rally to Absalom as one man, when in fact, once the first fever of revolution passed, a large part of the nation would gather again to David. Absalom and his party never so much as conceived the possibility — and in that blindness, K&D observe with Clericus, the plan's very boastfulness was what pleased the younger men, and the divine sentence took effect.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the two agencies side by side. He grants how much the policy of man can do — had Hushai not been there, Ahithophel's counsel would certainly have prevailed — yet insists on how much more the providence of God can do: Hushai managed the plot with dexterity, but the success is ascribed to God and his agency upon the minds of those concerned. Here was a wonderful effect of divine providence 'blinding Absalom's mind and influencing his heart,' so that he could not rest in Ahithophel's counsel. Henry notes the discriminating justice of it: Ahithophel's former counsel had been followed, for God meant to correct David; his latter counsel was not, for God meant not to destroy him.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley gathers the comfort of the doctrine for all who fear God: the Lord 'turns all men's hearts as the rivers of water'; he stands in the congregation of the mighty, holds an overruling hand in all counsels and a negative voice in all resolves, and laughs at men's projects against his children. The reformed Gill adds only that the elders who had just applauded Ahithophel now reversed themselves — a reversal the text itself explains by the hand of God. The witnesses do not divide: human craft and divine sovereignty meet in this one sentence, and the whole weight falls on the second, for David's prayer is answered and the wisest head in Israel is turned to foolishness at the appointed hour.
15:
The scene shifts from the council to the courier network, and the witnesses attend to Hushai's swift passing of intelligence to David through the priests. Keil and Delitzsch report the message plainly: Hushai told Zadok and Abiathar both what Ahithophel had counselled and what he himself had advised, and urged that David be warned without delay — 'Lodge not this night by the fords of the wilderness, but rather pass over, lest the king and all the people be swallowed up.' The urgency, the grammatical-historical reading stresses, is real: Hushai could not be certain his own counsel would hold, and prudence demanded that David act as if Ahithophel's swifter plan might yet be revived.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the passage into a study of providence guarding its own by humble means. He supposes Hushai was dismissed from the council before the final resolve and so feared the king might yet be swallowed up; therefore he provided against the worst and hurried the intelligence out. Henry marvels at the frailty of the instruments God employs: the young priests must slip secretly out of the city by En-rogel, for it had come to this in Jerusalem that two faithful priests dared not be seen; the message is entrusted to 'a poor simple young woman,' yet providence can make an ignorant girl a trusty messenger and serve its wise counsels 'by the foolish things of the world.' When the couriers are spotted and hidden in a well at Bahurim, and the woman of the house covers the mouth with a cloth and spreads corn upon it, Henry follows every turn — and pauses at her later lie to say honestly, 'We must not do evil that good may come of it.'

The reformed Gill supplies the connecting fact: Hushai, as soon as he returned from the council board, went straight to Zadok and Abiathar, whom he knew to be in David's interest and appointed to transmit whatever passed in Jerusalem. Henry adds the tradition that on this flight, looking back from the land of Jordan, David penned the forty-second Psalm (Ps. 42:6). The witnesses are united in the lesson: the deliverance of the Lord's anointed hung on a whispered warning and a servant-girl's errand, and the God who overruled the council now guarded the message it produced.
16:
Hushai's message to David is all urgency, and the witnesses weigh both the counsel and the fear beneath it. The grammatical-historical Ellicott explains why speed was everything: Hushai's advice had been adopted for the moment, but a council that had swung one way could swing back, and Ahithophel's swifter plan might at any hour be revived; there was instant danger for David, and Hushai urges him to put the Jordan without delay between himself and the rebels. Keil and Delitzsch give the words — 'Lodge not this night in the plains of the wilderness, but speedily pass over, lest the king be swallowed up, and all the people that are with him' — and locate the couriers, Jonathan and Ahimaaz, waiting at the Rogel spring outside the city.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley puts his finger on the precise anxiety that drives the warning: 'lest the king's and people's minds change, and Ahithophel persuade the king to pursue you speedily.' The peril is not that Hushai's counsel has failed but that it might yet be reversed, and David must not stake his life on the steadiness of a rebel council. The prudent course is to act on the worst case, cross the river, and be beyond reach before any change of mind can take effect.

The reformed Gill simply opens the message — 'now therefore send quickly, and tell David' — what had been advised on both sides. The witnesses do not divide; together they show a loyalty that will not rest in a single success but presses David to safety, and they draw the quiet lesson that even a providence which has just wrought a great deliverance does not dispense the delivered from ordinary vigilance. Gill observes that Hushai's very haste is itself a measure of how little he trusted the permanence of his victory in the council, and how much the king's life still hung in the balance. Hushai had overthrown Ahithophel in the council; now he labours as hard to see the fruit of it secured, sending word by night that the king might be over Jordan before the morning. The reader feels the narrowness of the margin: a few hours' delay, or a single change of Absalom's mind, and the warning would have come too late.
17:
The narrative pauses on the mechanics of the secret mission, and the witnesses spend their care on two details — the place En-rogel and the servant-girl who carried the word. The grammatical-historical Ellicott identifies the spot as a fountain just outside the city, on the boundary between Benjamin and Judah, and notes the two rival sites that have been proposed — the 'Fountain of the Virgin' on the western slope of the Kidron and 'Job's Well' below the meeting of the valleys — while observing that the loyalty of the high priests to David was well known, so that it would have been quite unsafe for their sons to set out as couriers from the city itself; even so they were pursued. Their station at the fountain, where women came to draw water, gave cover for the message to pass unremarked. Keil and Delitzsch place the same Rogel spring at the south-east corner of Jerusalem.

The word rendered 'wench' draws a long note from Adam Clarke, and the witnesses let him play the philologist. En-rogel, he explains, is the 'fullers' well,' from ein, a well, and regel, the foot, because the fullers trod the clothes there with their feet. As for 'wench' — a word found nowhere else in the English Bible — the Hebrew simply means girl, maid, or maidservant; Clarke canvasses its Anglo-Saxon and Gothic roots and supposes that King James, who elsewhere restored homely English words, restored this one too. Ellicott agrees that the definite article points to 'the maid-servant,' probably a known maid of the high priest's household.

The reformed Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Wesley fill in the ruse: the girl went to the well as if to wash clothes or draw water, and so carried the intelligence without suspicion. Gill notes the Targum's name for the place, the fuller's fountain, and the derivation of Rogel from the treading of the feet. The witnesses agree that the whole apparatus of the escape — the fountain on the border, the drawing of water, the unnoticed maid — is providence working through the most ordinary things, hiding the rescue of a king inside the daily errand of a servant.
18:
The couriers are spotted, and the witnesses trace the narrow escape into the well at Bahurim. The reformed Gill supplies the circumstance, drawing on Josephus: a lad saw Jonathan and Ahimaaz in conversation with the girl and marked their departure toward David's camp, and according to the historian these were horsemen who spied the couriers about a quarter-mile from Jerusalem and carried word to Absalom. The thread of the whole rescue hangs, at this moment, on a single boy's glance. Gill adds, on the same authority, that the couriers were spied when they had gone only about two furlongs from the city, so that the alarm was raised almost as soon as they set out.

Keil and Delitzsch fill in what follows and why. Absalom, they judge, had most likely set spies to watch the priests and their sons; so the two young men, noticing that they were observed, hurried into the house of a man at Bahurim who had a well — a cistern dry at the season — in his court, and went down into it. The grammatical-historical reading fixes the geography and the practical detail: Bahurim lay on the road eastward from Jerusalem toward the Jordan, the very village where David had lately been cursed by Shimei, and the dry summer cistern offered exactly the hiding-place the fugitives needed.

The verse is spare, but its place in the chapter is charged. Everything now depends on concealment: if Jonathan and Ahimaaz are taken, Hushai's warning dies with them and David, still on the near side of Jordan, may be overtaken. The witnesses, few and agreed, let the bare facts carry the suspense — the watchful boy, the swift turn off the road, the descent into the well — and quietly prepare the reader for the next verse, where a woman's quick wit will cover the well's mouth and turn the pursuers aside. Providence, which had blinded the council and moved the message out of the city, now hides its couriers in a hole in the ground, so that the safety of the anointed king rests, under God, on a dry cistern and a householder's loyalty.
19:
A woman's quick device saves the couriers, and the witnesses examine both her act and the rare word for what she spread. The reformed Gill identifies her as, very probably, the man of the house's wife, who took a cloth and spread it over the well's mouth so that it could not be seen or known that a well was there. The concealment is complete: to any pursuer the courtyard shows only an ordinary domestic scene.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the covering and the corn. Keil and Delitzsch note that she spread over the opening the covering she had close at hand and scattered ripoth upon it — peeled or bruised grain, the same word that appears in Prov. 27:22 — so that nothing was noticed, and they commend the Vulgate's rendering, 'as if drying peeled barley.' Ellicott confirms that this term for 'ground corn' occurs elsewhere only in that proverb and means wheat or barley beaten so as to remove the hull, then spread out to dry. The detail is not idle: grain laid to dry on a cloth is precisely the sort of common household work that would draw no second glance. Wesley's small inference is characteristic of the grammatical care the witnesses bring even to a household detail: the very corn on the cloth becomes a silent witness to the season, and so to the dryness of the well that hides the men below.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley catches the incidental confirmation of season the scene provides: the corn was spread under pretence of drying it in the sun, which shows, he observes, that it was summer-time — the same season that would have left the cistern dry enough to hide two men. The witnesses stand together on the surface facts and let the moral tension wait: the woman's stratagem so far is admirable presence of mind, an innocent cover thrown over a hidden rescue. It is only her word to the pursuers in the next verse that will raise the harder question of the lie; here the commentators are content to admire the deft hand that turned a courtyard and a handful of grain into a screen for the deliverance of the king's messengers, and through them of the king himself.
20:
The pursuers arrive and are turned aside by the woman's word, and the witnesses divide gently over how to judge it. The grammatical-historical commentators handle the peculiar phrase first. Keil and Delitzsch render her answer, 'they have gone over the little water-brook' — a term (mikal hammayim) found nowhere else — by which she 'led them wrong, so that they did not find them.' Ellicott confirms that this rare word for brook occurs only here and is thought by some to be a proper name, a small brook still bearing the name Michal being reported in the locality; and he sets the woman's deceit beside the like acts of Rahab hiding the spies (Josh. 2:4–7) and Michal covering David's flight (1 Sam. 19:12–17), noting that the historian 'simply records without comment what was done.'

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley is blunter: her words that they had gone 'over the brook of water' meant, in effect, over Jordan, and 'this was a manifest lie.' He does not soften it, and here the witnesses feel the moral pinch. The rescue of the couriers — and with them the safety of the anointed king — was purchased by a falsehood, and the commentators refuse to pretend otherwise. Matthew Henry, surveying the episode, had already drawn the governing rule that we may not 'do evil that good may come of it,' honouring the woman's courage while declining to justify her lie.

The reformed Gill keeps to the narrative, marking how the servants, having tracked the couriers to the house, were sent off empty on a false trail and returned to Jerusalem. The witnesses do not finally quarrel: they agree on the fact that God used the episode to preserve his people, and they agree too that the Scripture reports the woman's deception without endorsing it — leaving the deed, as with Rahab, to be weighed by the reader against the plain law of truth. The deliverance is real and providential; the means, on the point of the lie, is left honestly unpraised, so that the mercy of the outcome is never made a cover for the fault in the method.
21:
The danger past, the couriers deliver their warning and David crosses the Jordan, and the witnesses read the verse as the swift, quiet fulfilment of Hushai's whole labour. Keil and Delitzsch report the scene: when the pursuers had gone, the priests' sons came up out of the well and brought David the news, 'Arise, and pass quickly over the water: for thus hath Ahithophel counselled against you'; whereupon David and all the people with him went hastily over Jordan. The grammatical-historical reading lingers on the last touch of the Hebrew — that by the morning light 'there lacked not one of them that was not gone over Jordan,' not so much as one man missed — a note the commentators mark as proof of how complete and orderly the night's crossing was.

The message the couriers carry is itself significant. They name Ahithophel's counsel as the thing to be feared — the swift night-pursuit that Hushai had overturned but that David could not be sure was dead — and so David acts on the worst case, exactly as Hushai had urged, and puts the river between himself and any change of the rebels' mind. The reformed Gill anchors the sequence simply: this took place after Absalom's servants had departed, the warning David had prayed for (15:31) now reaching him in time.

The witnesses, few and agreed, let the verse stand as the pivot between peril and safety. Every strand of the preceding drama — the overruled council, the whispered warning, the maid at the fountain, the boy who saw, the well and its covering, the false trail — converges here in a single decisive act: the king crosses the water and is beyond reach before dawn. The quiet arithmetic of the closing clause, that not one was left behind, is the narrator's way of saying that the deliverance was entire; the God who had appointed to defeat Ahithophel's counsel has now carried his anointed and every soul with him safely over Jordan, so that the rebellion, when at last it musters and pursues, will find only an empty bank.
22:
David and all his people pass over Jordan, and the witnesses fasten on the little clause that not one was left behind. Keil and Delitzsch note the completeness the Hebrew presses — by the morning light 'there lacked not one of them that was not gone over Jordan' — and the reformed Gill adds only that David did this in taking the advice of Hushai, crossing the river as the warning had urged.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the most of the detail and turns it toward Christ. He observes that David and all with him went over in the night, whether by the ferry-boats that plied there or through the fords the text does not say; but special notice is taken that 'there lacked not one of them.' None deserted him though his distress was great; none stayed behind sick or weary; none were lost or cast away in passing the river. Herein, Henry says, some make David a type of the Messiah, who could say in a difficult day, 'Of all that thou hast given me have I lost none' (John 18:9; compare John 17:12). The shepherd whom Ahithophel had hoped to smite brings his whole flock over the water intact.

Henry follows David on to Mahanaim, a Levites' city on the far border of Gad, not far from Rabbah, which Ishbosheth had once made his royal seat and which David now makes his headquarters, having at last the time to raise an army against the rebels (v.24). The grammatical-historical reading underscores the strategic sense of the move: the Jordan is now a barrier between David and his pursuers, and the fortified town beyond it a rallying-point for the adherents who will gather once the first fever of the revolt has cooled. The witnesses do not divide; they read the crossing as at once a deliverance and a type — the anointed king, driven out but not forsaken, brought safe with all his own to the ground where his cause will be restored, losing not one of those the Lord had given him.
23:
Ahithophel, his counsel slighted, goes home and hangs himself, and the witnesses read his end as at once a shrewd man's calculation, a proud man's collapse, and the visible justice of God. The grammatical-historical commentators stress the cold deliberation of it. Keil and Delitzsch observe that he foresaw Absalom would lose his cause through rejecting his advice, and that it would then be all over with himself; in his death David's prayer (15:31) was fulfilled. Ellicott presses the same reading: Ahithophel was moved not merely by chagrin at the rejection of his counsel but was shrewd enough to see that, with this fatal delay, Absalom's rebellion would inevitably fail and he himself be likely to come to a traitor's death.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke insists the act was no madness: this self-murder could not be called lunacy, for every step to it was deliberate — he foresaw Absalom's ruin, would not witness and share the disgrace, and could expect no mercy from David; 'a very bad man,' he died 'an unprepared and accursed death.' Wesley draws the double moral the deed forces: here is 'contempt poured upon the wisdom of man,' for the most renowned politician that ever lived played the fool with himself more than any; and here is 'honour done to the justice of God,' for the wicked is snared in the work of his own hands.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry probes the two springs of the suicide. His pride could not bear that another's opinion was preferred, and, thinking himself the only wise man, he would die that wisdom might die with him; and his fear told him that, Absalom's cause being lost, he as the greatest criminal must answer to David's justice. Henry marvels at the deliberation — he went home to his house, set his affairs in order, made his will as a man of sound memory, and yet had not sense to suspend the sentence his passion had passed on his own neck. Ahithophel, whose name Henry glosses as 'the brother of a fool,' proves that nothing indicates so much folly as self-murder (Eccl. 8:10); and Henry adds, with Lightfoot, that David likely penned the fifty-fifth Psalm of this equal and guide turned traitor (Ps. 55:13), so that the prayer 'let death seize upon them' (Ps. 55:15; compare Ps. 7:15) was answered. The reformed Gill notes only that Ahithophel acted when he saw Hushai's counsel followed and not his. The witnesses converge: the wisest head in Israel dies as a fool, and the breath is stopped from which nothing but mischief could come.
24:
The scene shifts to the two armies taking their ground, and the witnesses attend to Mahanaim and to the timing of Absalom's pursuit. The reformed Gill dwells on the place: Mahanaim was a city beyond Jordan in the tribe of Gad, famous as the spot where the angels of God met Jacob (Gen. 32:2), and for a time the royal seat of Ishbosheth the son of Saul. The name, 'two camps,' and its sacred memory make it a fitting refuge for the driven king. Gill notes that the name recalls the double host of angels that met Jacob at the spot, so that the place carried for Israel a standing memory of divine encampment and protection — a fit ground on which a hunted anointed king might turn and stand.

The grammatical-historical commentators secure its strategic value. Keil and Delitzsch describe it as a fortified city east of the Jordan, not far from the ford of the Jabbok (compare 2 Sam. 2:8), where David could gather and order his forces; Ellicott notes that the very reasons which once made it a favourable capital for Ish-bosheth now recommended it as a place of refuge for David and a rallying-point for his adherents. Behind a river and within strong walls, the king who had crossed Jordan with a weary band could begin to become an army again.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley fixes the other half of the verse — that Absalom 'passed over Jordan, he and all the men of Israel with him.' The crossing was not made speedily, Wesley observes, but only when all the men of Israel had been gathered together according to Hushai's counsel. The point is quietly decisive: Absalom's delay is the direct fruit of the advice God had inclined him to prefer, and it is precisely this slow muster, so flattering to his vanity, that gives David the interval he needs. The witnesses do not divide; together they show the providence of the previous verses now working itself out in geography and in time — the anointed king safe behind Jordan in a walled and hallowed city, the usurper crossing after him only when the fatal delay has already done its silent work.
25:
The narrative pauses to name Absalom's new general and untangle his lineage, and the witnesses spend their care on two textual knots. Keil and Delitzsch set the frame: Absalom made Amasa captain over his army in place of Joab, who had stayed true to David and gone with him to Mahanaim; Amasa was son of a man named Jithra by Abigail, daughter of Nahash and sister of Zeruiah, and so Joab's illegitimate cousin. The first knot is the description of Jithra as 'an Israelite,' which K&D find so pointless a remark that they prefer, with 1 Chron. 2:17, the reading 'an Ishmaelite' — the Septuagint having 'Jezreelite' only by conjecture. Ellicott concurs: Jether and Ithra are merely forms of one name, and 'Israelite' is probably an error for 'Ishmeelite,' the Alexandrian Greek reading Ishmaelite and the Vatican Jezreelite.

The second knot is the parentage of Abigail. Ellicott lays it out: since this Abigail is called 'sister to Zeruiah,' and in 1 Chron. 2:16 both Abigail and Zeruiah are named sisters of Jesse's sons, it follows either that 'sister' is used loosely for half-sister, or else that Nahash — usually a man's name — was the name of Jesse's wife. He judges it impossible to decide with certainty, and dismisses the Jewish tradition that Nahash is simply another name for Jesse as without support.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws the family conclusion the genealogy yields: from this verse it appears that Joab and Amasa were sisters' children, and both nephews to David. The reformed Gill states the plain fact that opens the verse — that Amasa was set over the host in Joab's room, Joab being away with David. The witnesses, all of the grammatical-historical temper here save Gill, do not contend over doctrine; their labour is to read the text honestly, to prefer the better manuscript where the received word puzzles, and to own where the evidence will not yield a certain answer. The human note beneath the philology is not lost: Absalom's commander is David's own kinsman, so that the civil war is, to its very generals, a house divided against itself.
26:
The two forces encamp for battle, and the witnesses fix the ground and mark the first sign that David is not wholly forsaken. The reformed Gill locates the muster: 'so Israel and Absalom pitched in the land of Gilead,' the territory of Reuben and Gad and the half-tribe of Manasseh, where the Israelites with Absalom encamped to give battle to David and his men. The rebel host has now crossed into the very region to which the king has fled.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott maps Gilead more fully as the tract east of the Jordan reaching from Moab in the south to Bashan in the north, and adds a point of some weight for the story to come: though the site of Mahanaim has not been certainly identified, it lay almost certainly within Gilead, and Absalom did not in fact reach Mahanaim before he met and was defeated by David's forces. The encampment in Gilead is thus the last stage before the battle that will end the revolt. Keil and Delitzsch, reading on into the next verse, mark the counter-sign: when David came to Mahanaim, some of the wealthier citizens east of Jordan supplied his men with provisions — the first token that the people had not all fallen away, but that men of standing still held to him.

The verse is brief, but its place in the chapter is pointed. Hushai's counsel had promised Absalom the gathered strength of all Israel, and here that strength is at last assembled and pitched in Gilead; yet the same delay that mustered it has given David his walled refuge and the beginnings of an army, and the loyalty of the Gileadite notables is about to show how hollow was the assumption that the whole nation stood with the usurper. The commentators mark how the geography itself frames the coming battle: the rebel army, mustered at last in Gilead, stands on the very ground where David's restored fortunes will be secured, and where Absalom will fall. The witnesses do not divide; together they set the stage — two camps facing each other in the land of 'two camps' — and let the reader feel that the tide, under a providence already declared, has quietly begun to turn back toward the anointed king.
27:
David reaches Mahanaim and finds unlooked-for friends, and the witnesses read the arrival of Shobi, Machir, and Barzillai as providence repaying old kindness in the hour of need. The grammatical-historical Ellicott identifies them: Shobi, son of Nahash of Rabbah of the Ammonites, was very possibly a brother of that Hanun whom David had conquered (2 Sam. 10:1; 2 Sam. 12:29), perhaps left as governor over the conquered territory and now coming forward to show gratitude; while Machir the son of Ammiel is the very man who had once maintained the crippled son of Jonathan (2 Sam. 9:4), so that David 'now reaps a reward for his kindness.' Keil and Delitzsch add that these wealthier men of the east supplied David's company as the first proof that the people had not all deserted him.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads the same names in the same key: Shobi, it may seem, had disliked and disowned the barbarous insult his brother offered David's ambassadors, and so, when the rest of the royal house was destroyed, was left as king or governor over the remnant of Ammon — and Machir he simply refers back to the earlier kindness recorded of him. The pattern is unmistakable: those to whom David had shown mercy now become his supply.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the lesson. He notes that even Shobi, a younger brother of the Ammonite royal house, was kind to David, probably returning favours once received; and he draws the maxim that those whose prosperity seems most secure know not when they may need the kindness of such as now lie at their mercy, which is reason enough to do good to all men while we have opportunity, 'for he that watereth shall be watered also himself' (Prov. 11:25). Machir, once the patron of Mephibosheth, is here repaid; Barzillai we shall meet again. The reformed Gill marks only that this befell when David first came to Mahanaim. The witnesses do not divide; together they see in these three benefactors the God who 'sometimes makes up to his people that comfort from strangers which they are disappointed of in their own families,' turning a fugitive's exile into an occasion of unexpected friendship.
28:
The benefactors' gifts are catalogued, and the witnesses attend to the homely inventory of a camp's needs supplied. The reformed Gill opens the list with the beds — brought for David and his men, who, fleeing Jerusalem in haste, could carry none with them and were ill-provided in the plains of the wilderness — noting that the Septuagint reckoned ten of them, and of tapestry wrought on both sides, such as the ancients used. Keil and Delitzsch simply set down the whole provision: beds and basins and earthen vessels, wheat and barley and flour, parched corn, beans and lentils and parched pulse.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke turns antiquarian on the items, and the witnesses let him. The 'beds,' he supposes, were no more than skins of beasts, mats, and carpets; the 'basins' (sappoth) were probably wooden bowls such as the Arabs still use to eat from and to knead bread in; the 'earthen vessels' (keley yotser) were clay pots baked in the sun, for lifting water and boiling food. He observes that no flesh-meat is directly mentioned, little being eaten in that country and none keeping well, and illustrates the 'parched corn and parched pulse' from the practice of the Moors of Barbary, who make of parched barley-flour a chief provision for their journeys — carried, kneaded with honey or oil or water, in a leathern satchel that is itself the Scripture 'bottle.'

The grammatical-historical reading keeps the point of the catalogue in view: this is not incidental detail but the measure of a real deliverance. David's company had crossed Jordan weary and empty-handed, and here, unbidden, is everything an army in the field requires — furniture for rest, vessels for cooking, and grain and pulse for food. The witnesses do not divide; together they see in the loaded gifts of Shobi, Machir, and Barzillai the same providence that had overruled the council and guarded the couriers, now feeding the king it had delivered. Clarke reserves for the end of the chapter his long account of the she-bear robbed of her whelps, recalling the very image Hushai had used of David's men; but the plain sense of the verse is mercy in the form of bread, a table spread in the wilderness for a hungry and hunted king.
29:
The list of gifts closes with the richest fare, and with it the reason for all this kindness, and the witnesses land the chapter on the note of God's provision through strangers. The reformed Gill dwells on the honey and butter that head the verse: honey was much in use among the ancients — Homer names it among the provisions of a feast, and the old Scythians were said to live on milk and honey — and honey and butter were, with such fare, much the food of the people of Judea. To a company newly out of the wilderness, these were not luxuries but strength.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott handles the one obscure item, the 'cheese of kine.' The word occurs nowhere else and its meaning is uncertain; the English follows the Chaldee, Syriac, and Rabbinic interpretation, while the Vulgate renders it 'fat calves' and Theodotion 'sucking calves.' The commentators grant that the exact dish cannot be recovered, but the drift is plain: the produce of the herd, in whatever form, was added to the grain and the honey to make a full table. The whole is gathered in the verse's own explanation — that they brought it 'for David, and for the people that were with him, to eat: for they said, The people is hungry, and weary, and thirsty, in the wilderness.'

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley presses that closing clause into the chapter's pastoral conclusion. It was because David and his men had been 'in the wilderness' — hungry, weary, thirsty — that these friends brought their stores; and in it Wesley reads the settled comfort that God 'sometimes makes up to his people that comfort from strangers which they are disappointed of in their own families.' The point is exact for David, betrayed by a son and abandoned by his people, yet fed by an Ammonite prince and two men of Gilead. The witnesses do not divide; they close the account of the flight where it began, under the hand of providence — the God who had turned the council, guarded the couriers, and carried the king over Jordan now spreads him a table in the wilderness, and teaches that kindness shown to the least of his afflicted is kindness he himself receives and repays.
